title: Soft pretzels
subtitle: German-style yeasted pretzels dipped in baking soda solution and baked with coarse salt
excerpt: Soft, chewy pretzels with the characteristic dark, shiny crust produced by dipping the shaped dough in a hot baking soda solution before baking. A true laugenbrezel uses a lye (sodium hydroxide) bath, which is not practical at home; a concentrated baking soda solution baked briefly first gives a close result. The interior is soft and bread-like. Serve warm with German mustard.
sourceNotes: Pretzels (Brezeln or Laugenbrezel) have been made in German-speaking regions since at least the early medieval period. Their distinctive knotted shape and alkaline-bath treatment are documented in illustrations and culinary records from the thirteenth century onwards. The home-baking adaptation using a baking soda solution in place of lye is widely documented in twentieth-century German home-baking literature, including Luise Holle, Das Grosse Einmaleins der guten Küche (1970s editions). The baked baking soda method (which increases alkalinity) is credited to J. Kenji Lopez-Alt in The Food Lab (2015), which is a separately published commercial source.

content.text: The dark, glossy crust comes from the

: the shaped pretzels are dipped briefly in hot baking soda solution before baking. The dough itself is a firm, low-hydration bread dough with a short bulk ferment; there is no final proof after shaping. The pretzels go straight from the bath into the oven.

Combine the flour, yeast, salt, and sugar. Rub in the softened butter. Add the warm water and mix to a firm, smooth dough. Knead for 8 to 10 minutes until elastic. The dough should be firmer than a typical bread dough and not sticky. Place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and leave to rise in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled.

Line two baking trays with greased baking paper. Divide the dough into 8 equal pieces (about 105g each). Roll each piece into a rope about 60 cm long, thicker in the centre and tapering at the ends. Form the classic pretzel shape: lift the two ends, cross them twice, and press them down onto the thick arch. Arrange on the trays. Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional (200°C fan).

Bring 1 litre of water to the boil in a medium saucepan. Add the bicarbonate of soda carefully (it will foam briefly). Reduce to a steady simmer. Working in batches of two or three, lower each pretzel into the solution and hold for 30 seconds, then lift out with a slotted spoon and return to the tray. Sprinkle with flaky salt. Slash the thick part of each pretzel once with a sharp blade. Bake for 18 to 22 minutes until deep mahogany brown all over. Cool on a rack for at least 10 minutes before eating.
